revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''度会氏'''（わたらいし）は、[[明治]]初期に[[伊勢豊受大神宮]]（[[伊勢神宮]]外宮）の祠官を[[世襲]]した氏族。 度会氏...'
revision.text:
'''度会氏'''（わたらいし）は、[[明治]]初期に[[伊勢豊受大神宮]]（[[伊勢神宮]]外宮）の祠官を[[世襲]]した氏族。
度会氏は、天牟羅雲命を祖とする伝えられ、当初は磯部を称していたが、[[奈良時代]]に渡相姓を下賜されたという。古くから伊勢神宮における[[禰宜]]を世襲し、[[鎌倉時代]]後期から[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]にかけて、[[伊勢皇大神宮]]（内宮）と争い[[伊勢神道]]を唱えた。多くの家に分かれたが、禰宜になれる家（重代家）は、檜垣家・松木家など6家のみであった。
==関連項目== *[[社家]]
[[category:日本の氏族|わたらいし]] [[category:神職|わたらいし]]

'''度会氏'''（わたらいし）は、[[明治]]初期まで[[伊勢豊受大神宮]]（[[伊勢神宮]]外宮）の祠官を[[世襲]]した氏族。
度会氏は、天牟羅雲命を祖とする伝えられ、当初は磯部を称していたが、[[奈良時代]]に渡相姓を下賜されたという。古くから伊勢神宮における[[禰宜]]を世襲し、[[鎌倉時代]]後期から[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]にかけて、[[伊勢皇大神宮]]（内宮）と争い[[伊勢神道]]を唱えた。多くの家に分かれたが、禰宜になれる家（重代家）は、檜垣家・松木家など6家のみであった。
==関連項目== *[[社家]]
[[category:日本の氏族|わたらいし]] [[category:神職|わたらいし]]

度会氏は、天牟羅雲命を祖とする伝えられ、当初は磯部を称していたが、[[奈良時代]]に渡相姓を下賜されたという。古くから伊勢神宮における[[禰宜]]を世襲し、[[鎌倉時代]]後期から[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]にかけて、[[伊勢皇大神宮]]（内宮）と争い[[伊勢神道]]を唱えた。多くの家に分かれたが、禰宜になれる家（重代家）は、檜垣家・松木家など6家のみであった。

[[category:日本の氏族|わたらいし]] [[category:神職|わたらいし]]

度会氏は、[[天村雲命|天牟羅雲命]]を祖とすると伝えられ、当初は磯部を称していたが、[[奈良時代]]に渡相姓を下賜されたという。古くから伊勢神宮における[[禰宜]]を世襲し、[[鎌倉時代]]後期から[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]にかけて、[[伊勢皇大神宮]]（内宮）と争い[[伊勢神道]]を唱えた。多くの家に分かれたが、禰宜になれる家（重代家）は、檜垣家・松木家など6家のみであった。

[[宮後家]]（社家）など多数。 }} '''度会氏'''（わたらいうじ）は、[[日本]]の[[氏族]]。多くは[[明治]]初期まで[[豊受大神宮|伊勢豊受大神宮]]（[[伊勢神宮]]外宮）の祠官を[[世襲]]した。 == 概要 == 度会氏の祖は[[天牟羅雲命]]であると伝えられ、当初は[[磯部]]を称していたが、[[奈良時代]]に渡会（渡相）神主姓を下賜されたという。古くから伊勢神宮外宮の[[禰宜]]を世襲し、[[鎌倉時代]]後期から[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]にかけて、[[伊勢皇大神宮]]（内宮）と争い[[伊勢神道]]を唱えた。多くの家に分かれたが、禰宜を務められた家（重代家）は、檜垣家・松木家など6家のみであった。 == 主な度会氏 == * [[度会行忠]] * [[度会家行]] == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author=日本史用語研究会|authorlink=|title=必携日本史用語|origdate=2009-2-2|accessdate=|edition=四訂版|publisher=[[実教出版]]|series=|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|authorlink=|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|origdate=2009-3-30|accessdate=|edition=改訂版|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|series=|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=|authorlink=|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|origdate=2005-2-25|accessdate=|edition=|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|学研]]|series=|isbn=9784056037753}} == 関連項目 == * [[社家]] * [[荒木田氏]] * [[神道五部書]] {{神道　横}} {{デフォルトソート:わたらいうし}} [[category:度会氏|*]] [[Category:日本の氏族]] [[Category:近畿地方の氏族]] [[Category:東海地方の氏族]] [[Category:社家]] [[category:度会郡]] [[Category:伊勢神宮]] [[Category:伊勢市の歴史]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=三重県}}

revision.text:
{{脚注の不足|date=June 2017}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | Clan name = Watarai clan | Crest description = Flower diamond crest, Sixteen-petal chrysanthemum crest | Original surname = [[Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ise no Ason]] | Ancestor = [[Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Mikoto]] | Type = [[Shinbetsu]] (descendants of heavenly or earthly deities)
[[Kōbetsu]]
[[Kazoku]] (Barons) | Place of origin = [[Ise Province]] or [[Tanba Province]] | Base = [[Watarai District]], [[Ise Province]] | Key figures = [[Watarai Haruhiko]]
[[Watarai Ieyuki]]
[[Deguchi Nobuyoshi]] | Branches = [[Higaki family]] (shrine family)
Matsuki family (shrine family)
[[Hisamoto family]] (shrine family)
[[Sakume family]] (shrine family)
[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[Meiji period]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Jingū]] in a [[hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to [[Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Mikoto]] (son of [[Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto]] or [[Ame-no-Mikumo-no-Mikoto]]), and they are considered descendants of the [[Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ise no Ason]][[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[Kamakura period]] and [[Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Jingū|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Watarai}} [[Category:Watarai clan]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of the Kinki region]] [[Category:Clans of the Tōkai region]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Ise Jingū]] [[Category:History of Ise City]] {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}}

[[Kazoku]] (Barons) | Place of origin = [[Ise Province]] or [[Tanba Province]] | Base = [[Watarai District]], [[Ise Province]] | Key figures = [[Watarai Haruhiko]]
[[Watarai Ieyuki]]
[[Deguchi Nobuyoshi]] | Branches = [[Higaki family]] (shrine family)
Matsuki family (shrine family)
[[Hisamoto family]] (shrine family)
[[Sakume family]] (shrine family)
[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[Meiji period]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Jingū]] in a [[hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to [[Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Mikoto]] (son of [[Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto]] or [[Ame-no-Mikumo-no-Mikoto]]), and they are considered descendants of the [[Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ise no Ason]][[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[Kamakura period]] and [[Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Jingū|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Watarai}} [[Category:Watarai clan]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of the Kinki region]] [[Category:Clans of the Tōkai region]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Ise Jingū]] [[Category:History of Ise City]] [[Category:Kk]][[Category:New pages]][[category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[category:original pages]][[Category:newer pages]][[ja:度会氏]] {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}}

While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Watarai}} [[Category:Watarai clan]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of the Kinki region]] [[Category:Clans of the Tōkai region]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Ise Jingū]] [[Category:History of Ise City]] [[Category:Kk]][[Category:New pages]][[category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:dardar]] [[category:most recent 500]][[ja:度会氏]] {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}}

Matsuki family (shrine family)
[[Hisamoto family]] (shrine family)
[[Sakume family]] (shrine family)
[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[Meiji period]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Jingū]] in a [[hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to [[Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Mikoto]] (son of [[Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto]] or [[Ame-no-Mikumo-no-Mikoto]]), and they are considered descendants of the [[Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ise no Ason]][[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[Kamakura period]] and [[Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Jingū|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Watarai}} [[Category:Watarai clan]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of the Kinki region]] [[Category:Clans of the Tōkai region]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Ise Jingū]] [[Category:History of Ise City]] [[Category:Kk]][[Category:New pages]][[category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:dardar]] [[category:most recent 500]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:度会氏]] {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}}

[[Kazoku]] (Barons) | Place of origin = [[Ise Province]] or [[Tanba Province]] | Base = [[Watarai District]], [[Ise Province]] | Key figures = [[Watarai Haruhiko]]
[[Watarai Ieyuki]]
[[Deguchi Nobuyoshi]] | Branches = [[Higaki family]] (shrine family)
Matsuki family (shrine family)
[[Hisamoto family]] (shrine family)
[[Sakume family]] (shrine family)
[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[Meiji period]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Jingū]] in a [[hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to [[Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Mikoto]] (son of [[Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto]] or [[Ame-no-Mikumo-no-Mikoto]]), and they are considered descendants of the [[Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ise no Ason]][[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[Kamakura period]] and [[Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Jingū|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Watarai}} [[Category:Watarai clan]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of the Kinki region]] [[Category:Clans of the Tōkai region]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Ise Jingū]] [[Category:History of Ise City]] [[Category:Kk]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:度会氏]] {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}}

Until the early [[Meiji period]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Jingū]] in a [[hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to [[Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Mikoto]] (son of [[Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto]] or [[Ame-no-Mikumo-no-Mikoto]]), and they are considered descendants of the [[Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ise no Ason]][[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[Kamakura period]] and [[Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Jingū|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT::Watarai}} [[Category:Watarai clan]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of the Kinki region]] [[Category:Clans of the Tōkai region]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Ise Jingū]] [[Category:History of Ise City]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Watarai clan}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Watarai clan}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:度会氏]] {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}}

Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[Kamakura period]] and [[Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Jingū|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise Shintō]]. {{脚注の不足|date=June 2017}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | Clan name = Watarai clan | Crest description = Flower diamond crest, Sixteen-petal chrysanthemum crest | Original surname = [[Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ise no Ason]] | Ancestor = [[Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Mikoto]] | Type = [[Shinbetsu]] (descendants of heavenly or earthly deities)
[[Kōbetsu]]
[[Kazoku]] (Barons) | Place of origin = [[Ise Province]] or [[Tanba Province]] | Base = [[Watarai District]], [[Ise Province]] | Key figures = [[Watarai Haruhiko]]
[[Watarai Ieyuki]]
[[Deguchi Nobuyoshi]] | Branches = [[Higaki family]] (shrine family)
Matsuki family (shrine family)
[[Hisamoto family]] (shrine family)
[[Sakume family]] (shrine family)
[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[Meiji period]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Jingū]] in a [[hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to [[Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Mikoto]] (son of [[Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto]] or [[Ame-no-Mikumo-no-Mikoto]]), and they are considered descendants of the {{ill|Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊勢国造}}[[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[Kamakura period]] and [[Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Jingū|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * {{脚注の不足|date=June 2017}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | Clan name = Watarai clan | Crest description = Flower diamond crest, Sixteen-petal chrysanthemum crest | Original surname = [[Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ise no Ason]] | Ancestor = [[Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Mikoto]] | Type = [[Shinbetsu]] (descendants of heavenly or earthly deities)
[[Kōbetsu]]
[[Kazoku]] (Barons) | Place of origin = [[Ise Province]] or [[Tanba Province]] | Base = [[Watarai District]], [[Ise Province]] | Key figures = [[Watarai Haruhiko]]
[[Watarai Ieyuki]]
[[Deguchi Nobuyoshi]] | Branches = [[Higaki family]] (shrine family)
Matsuki family (shrine family)
[[Hisamoto family]] (shrine family)
[[Sakume family]] (shrine family)
[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[Meiji period]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Jingū]] in a [[hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to [[Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Mikoto]] (son of [[Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto]] or [[Ame-no-Mikumo-no-Mikoto]]), and they are considered descendants of the {{ill|Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊勢国造}}[[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[Kamakura period]] and [[Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Jingū|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite [[Kazoku]] (Barons) | Place of origin = [[Ise Province]] or [[Tanba Province]] | Base = [[Watarai District]], [[Ise Province]] | Key figures = [[Watarai Haruhiko]]
[[Watarai Ieyuki]]
[[Deguchi Nobuyoshi]] | Branches = [[Higaki family]] (shrine family)
Matsuki family (shrine family)
[[Hisamoto family]] (shrine family)
[[Sakume family]] (shrine family)
[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[Meiji period]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Jingū]] in a [[hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to [[Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Mikoto]] (son of [[Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto]] or [[Ame-no-Mikumo-no-Mikoto]]), and they are considered descendants of the {{ill|Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊勢国造}}[[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[Kamakura period]] and [[Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Jingū|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{citation needed|Watarai}} [[Category:Watarai clan]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of the Kinki region]] [[Category:Clans of the Tōkai region]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Ise Jingū]] [[Category:History of Ise City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Watarai clan}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[category:qq close]] [[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Watarai clan}} [[category:qq close]] [[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:atarai clan]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qqqq]] [[ja:度会氏]] {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:New]]

While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{citation needed|Watarai}} [[Category:Watarai clan]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of the Kinki region]] [[Category:Clans of the Tōkai region]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Ise Jingū]] [[Category:History of Ise City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Watarai clan}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:New uncategorized original categories]] [[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Watarai clan}} [[Category:New uncategorized original categories]] [[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[en:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:atarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qqqq]] [[ja:度会氏]] {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{脚注の不足|date=June 2017}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | Clan name = Watarai clan | Crest description = Flower diamond crest, Sixteen-petal chrysanthemum crest | Original surname = [[Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ise no Ason]] | Ancestor = [[Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Mikoto]] | Type = [[Shinbetsu]] (descendants of heavenly or earthly deities)
[[Kōbetsu]]
[[Kazoku]] (Barons) | Place of origin = [[Ise Province]] or [[Tanba Province]] | Base = [[Watarai District]], [[Ise Province]] | Key figures = [[Watarai Haruhiko]]
[[Watarai Ieyuki]]
[[Deguchi Nobuyoshi]] | Branches = [[Higaki family]] (shrine family)
Matsuki family (shrine family)
[[Hisamoto family]] (shrine family)
[[Sakume family]] (shrine family)
[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[Meiji period]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Jingū]] in a [[hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to [[Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Mikoto]] (son of [[Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto]] or [[Ame-no-Mikumo-no-Mikoto]]), and they are considered descendants of the {{ill|Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊勢国造}}[[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[Kamakura period]] and [[Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Jingū|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{citation needed|Watarai}} [[Category:Watarai clan]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of the Kinki region]] [[Category:Clans of the Tōkai region]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Ise Jingū]] [[Category:History of Ise City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Watarai clan}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:New uncategorized original categories]] [[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Watarai clan}} [[Category:New uncategorized original categories]] [[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[en:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:atarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qqqq]] [[ja:度会氏]] {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]]

While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{citation needed|Watarai}} [[Category:Watarai clan]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of the Kinki region]] [[Category:Clans of the Tōkai region]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Ise Jingū]] [[Category:History of Ise City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Watarai clan}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Watarai clan}} [[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[en:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:atarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:度会氏]] {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]]

[[Kōbetsu]]
[[Kazoku]] (Barons) | Place of origin = [[Ise Province]] or [[Tanba Province]] | Base = [[Watarai District]], [[Ise Province]] | Key figures = [[Watarai Haruhiko]]
[[Watarai Ieyuki]]
[[Deguchi Nobuyoshi]] | Branches = [[Higaki family]] (shrine family)
Matsuki family (shrine family)
[[Hisamoto family]] (shrine family)
[[Sakume family]] (shrine family)
[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[Meiji period]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Jingū]] in a [[hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to [[Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Mikoto]] (son of [[Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto]] or [[Ame-no-Mikumo-no-Mikoto]]), and they are considered descendants of the {{ill|Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊勢国造}}[[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[Kamakura period]] and [[Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Jingū|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{citation needed|Watarai}} [[Category:Watarai clan]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of the Kinki region]] [[Category:Clans of the Tōkai region]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Ise Jingū]] [[Category:History of Ise City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Watarai clan}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Watarai clan}} [[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[en:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:atarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:度会氏]] {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[Meiji period]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Jingū]] in a [[hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to [[Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Mikoto]] (son of [[Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto]] or [[Ame-no-Mikumo-no-Mikoto]]), and they are considered descendants of the {{ill|Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊勢国造|1=Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}[[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[Kamakura period]] and [[Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Jingū|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise Shintō]]. revision.text:
{{脚注の不足|date=June 2017}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | Clan name = Watarai clan | Crest description = Flower diamond crest, Sixteen-petal chrysanthemum crest | Original surname = [[Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ise no Ason]] | Ancestor = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命}} | Type = [[Shinbetsu]] (descendants of heavenly or earthly deities)
[[Kōbetsu]]
[[Kazoku]] (Barons) | Place of origin = [[Ise Province]] or [[Tanba Province]] | Base = [[Watarai District]], [[Ise Province]] | Key figures = [[Watarai Haruhiko]]
[[Watarai Ieyuki]]
[[Deguchi Nobuyoshi]] | Branches = [[Higaki family]] (shrine family)
Matsuki family (shrine family)
[[Hisamoto family]] (shrine family)
[[Sakume family]] (shrine family)
[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[Meiji period]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Jingū]] in a [[hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命}}, and they are considered descendants of the {{ill|Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊勢国造|1=Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}[[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[Kamakura period]] and [[Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Jingū|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{citation needed|Watarai}} [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:japanese clans]] {{citation needed|Watarai clan}} [[Category:japanese clans]][[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:japanese clans]]{{citation needed|Watarai Until the early [[Meiji period]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingu|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] in a [[:en:Hereditary|Hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, and they are considered descendants of the {{ill|Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊勢国造|1=Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}[[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingu," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise faith|Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|度会氏|version=103442621|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[sn:Watarai clan]] [[sn:atarai clan]] [[ja:度会氏]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:度会氏]] [[Category:度会郡]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:東海地方の氏族]] [[Category:Clans by region]]

While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|度会氏|version=103442621|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[sn:Watarai clan]] [[sn:atarai clan]] [[ja:度会氏]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai Clan]] [[Category:度会郡]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:Clans by region]]

Matsuki family (shrine family)
[[Hisamoto family]] (shrine family)
[[Sakume family]] (shrine family)
[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[:en:Clan|Clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingu|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] in a [[:en:Hereditary|Hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, and they are considered descendants of the {{ill|Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊勢国造|1=Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}[[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingu," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise faith|Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|度会氏|version=103442621|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[sn:Watarai clan]] [[sn:atarai clan]] [[ja:度会氏]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai Clan]] [[Category:度会郡]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:Clans by region]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11486123]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{More footnotes needed|date=June 2017}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | Clan name = Watarai clan | Crest description = Flower diamond crest, Sixteen-petal chrysanthemum crest | Original surname = [[Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ise no Ason]] | Ancestor = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}} | Type = [[Shinbetsu]] (descendants of heavenly or earthly deities)
[[Kōbetsu]]
[[Kazoku]] (Barons) | Place of origin = [[Ise Province]] or [[Tanba Province]] | Base = [[Watarai District]], [[Ise Province]] | Key figures = [[Watarai Haruhiko]]
[[Watarai Ieyuki]]
[[Deguchi Nobuyoshi]] | Branches = [[Higaki family]] (shrine family)
Matsuki family (shrine family)
[[Hisamoto family]] (shrine family)
[[Sakume family]] (shrine family)
[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[:en:Clan|Clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingu|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] in a [[:en:Hereditary|Hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, and they are considered descendants of the {{ill|Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊勢国造|1=Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}[[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingu," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise faith|Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|度会氏|version=103442621|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[sn:Watarai clan]] [[sn:atarai clan]] [[ja:度会氏]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai Clan]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:Clans by region]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11486123]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{More footnotes needed|date=June 2017}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | Clan name = Watarai clan | Crest description = Flower diamond crest, Sixteen-petal chrysanthemum crest | Original surname = [[Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ise no Ason]] | Ancestor = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}} | Type = [[Shinbetsu]] (descendants of heavenly or earthly deities)
[[Kōbetsu]]

[[Watarai Ieyuki]]
[[Deguchi Nobuyoshi]] | Branches = [[Higaki family]] (shrine family)
Matsuki family (shrine family)
[[Hisamoto family]] (shrine family)
[[Sakume family]] (shrine family)
[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[:en:Clan|Clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyouke Daijingu|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] in a [[:en:Hereditary|Hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, and they are considered descendants of the {{ill|Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊勢国造|1=Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}[[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyouke Daijingu," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise faith|Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|度会氏|version=103442621|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[sn:Watarai clan]] [[sn:atarai clan]] [[ja:度会氏]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai Clan]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:Clans by region]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Watarai clan]][[Category:qq]]

Until the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyōke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] in a [[:en:Hereditary|Hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, and they are considered descendants of the {{ill|Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊勢国造|1=Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}[[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyōke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise faith|Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|度会氏|version=103442621|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Watarai clan]][[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[sn:Watarai clan]] [[sn:atarai clan]] [[ja:度会氏]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai Clan]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:Clans by region]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[sn:Watarai clan]][[en:Watarai clan]]

While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|度会氏|version=103442621|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[ja:度会氏]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai Clan]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:Clans by region]] {{wikidata link|Q11486123}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[Kōbetsu]]

[[Watarai Ieyuki]]

Matsuki family (shrine family)
[[Hisamoto family]] (shrine family)
[[Sakume family]] (shrine family)
[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[:en:Clan|Clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyōke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] in a [[:en:Hereditary|Hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, and they are considered descendants of the {{ill|Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊勢国造|1=Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}[[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyōke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise faith|Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|度会氏|version=103442621|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[ja:度会氏]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai Clan]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:Clans by region]] {{wikidata link|Q11486123}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|度会氏|version=103442621|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[ja:度会氏]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai Clan]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:Clans by region]] {{wikidata link|Q11486123}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] {{wikidata link|Q11486123}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[ja:度会氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11486123]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{More footnotes needed|date=June 2017}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | Clan name = Watarai clan | Crest description = Flower diamond crest, Sixteen-petal chrysanthemum crest | Original surname = [[Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ise no Ason]] | Ancestor = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}} | Type = [[Shinbetsu]] (descendants of heavenly or earthly deities)
[[Kōbetsu]]
[[Kazoku]] (Barons) | Place of origin = [[Ise Province]] or [[Tanba Province]] | Base = [[Watarai District]], [[Ise Province]] | Key figures = [[Watarai Haruhiko]]
[[Watarai Ieyuki]]
[[Deguchi Nobuyoshi]] | Branches = [[Higaki family]] (shrine family)
Matsuki family (shrine family)
[[Hisamoto family]] (shrine family)
[[Sakume family]] (shrine family)
[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[:en:Clan|Clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyōke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] in a [[:en:Hereditary|Hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, and they are considered descendants of the {{ill|Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊勢国造|1=Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}[[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyōke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise faith|Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|度会氏|version=103442621|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[ja:度会氏]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai Clan]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:Clans by region]] {{wikidata link|Q11486123}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q11486123}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]]

[[Kawasaki family]] (shrine family)
[[Miyago family]] (shrine family) and others }} The '''Watarai clan''' (度会氏, Watarai-uji) is a Japanese [[:en:Clan|Clan]] whose family name is derived from "Watarai." Until the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], many members of this clan served as [[shrine priests]] (negi) of the [[Toyōke Daijingū|Gekū]] (Outer Shrine) of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] in a [[:en:Hereditary|Hereditary]] capacity. __TOC__ == Overview == According to widely circulated genealogies, the Watarai clan traces its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, and they are considered descendants of the {{ill|Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊勢国造|1=Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}[[Nishikawa Junto]] (ed.), "Watarai Shrine Priest Genealogical Record," in *Shintō Taikei: Ronsetsu-hen, Vol. 7 (Ise Shintō, Part 2)*, Shintō Taikei Compilation Society, 1982, pp. 415–417.. Another theory suggests that the Watarai clan came to Ise with [[Toyouke Ōmikami]], the deity enshrined at the Gekū, originally from [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]]Ikegaki Miyuki, "The Enshrinement and Traditions of Toyōke Daijingū," *The Formation and Historical Significance of the Nihon Shoki*, Nenshōsha, 2022, pp. 317–319. (Original article: *Koten to Rekishi*, No. 1, 2018).. The clan served as hereditary priests (negi) of the Gekū from ancient times. During the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], they challenged the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Naikū]] (Inner Shrine) and advocated [[Ise faith|Ise Shintō]]. While the clan divided into numerous branches, only six families, including the Higaki and Matsuki families, retained the privilege of serving as negi. == Notable Members of the Watarai Clan == * [[Watarai Yukitada]] * [[Watarai Ieyuki]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=必携日本史用語|author=日本史用語研究会|edition=4th ed.|publisher=[[実教出版]]|year=2009|isbn=9784407316599}} * {{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|edition=Revised ed.|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|title=神道行法の本―日本の霊統を貫く神祇奉祭の秘事|publisher=[[学研ホールディングス|Gakken]]|year=2005|isbn=9784056037753}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine families]] * [[Arakida clan]] * [[Shintō Gobusho]] {{Shintō Sidebar}} {{Shintō-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|Mie Prefecture}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|度会氏|version=103442621|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[ja:度会氏]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai Clan]] [[Category:Watarai District]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:Clans by region]] {{wikidata link|Q11486123}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q11486123}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]
